does: This paper applies sparse autoencoders to chemistry language models to extract and interpret latent features encoding chemical knowledge. The authors analyze the Foundation Models for Materials chemistry model by decomposing its hidden representations into sparse, interpretable components and examining how these features activate across molecular datasets. Their main contribution is demonstrating that CLMs develop semantically meaningful latent features corresponding to distinct chemical concepts including molecular structural motifs, physicochemical properties, and pharmacological information, providing concrete interpretability tools for black-box chemistry models.
connects: This work extends established sparse autoencoder interpretability techniques, recently popularized in large language model analysis, to the specialized domain of chemistry. It builds on prior efforts to demystify neural representations in chemistry models and responds to growing concerns about interpretability in high-stakes molecular design applications. The paper connects to broader research on feature extraction and mechanistic understanding of deep learning models, as well as work on foundation models for molecular tasks. It also relates to recent advances in making generative models more transparent for drug and material discovery pipelines.
enables: This interpretability framework enables safer deployment of chemistry language models in drug and material discovery by revealing what chemical concepts models actually learn and rely on. It opens pathways for detecting failure modes and spurious correlations in molecular predictions. The sparse feature representations could support mechanistic studies of how CLMs generalize across chemical space, inform better pretraining strategies, and facilitate debugging of model errors on critical tasks. Future work could use these interpretable features for knowledge distillation, improved model steering, or systematic evaluation of whether models capture domain-appropriate chemistry principles needed for real-world applications.
